primer.markdown:
# Transactional mail without the SMTP detour

BARQUE does not speak SMTP directly. It shells out to [Charm Pop](https://github.com/charmbracelet/pop), a single-binary CLI that already knows how to talk to Resend's HTTP API or fall back to SMTP on request. The integration lives in `barque/core/email.py`.

The contract is simple and mostly environmental: set `RESEND_API_KEY` in your shell, pick a verified `--from` address, and call `barque send doc.md --to someone@example.com`. BARQUE builds a `pop` command line (`--from`, `--to`, `--subject`, `--attach`), injects `RESEND_API_KEY` into the subprocess environment via `_get_env_vars()`, and hands off. Pop takes it from there.

Why Resend instead of your company's SMTP relay? Two reasons, both load-bearing for a solo builder. First, SMTP credentials are the highest-value secret on your machine — Resend lets you rotate a narrow-scope API key instead. Second, Resend ships a free tier that only delivers to your verified email (BARQUE's `README.md` calls this out explicitly). That constraint is a feature: it makes Resend safe as a development dependency without a domain verification ritual, and you can promote to full-domain delivery by verifying at resend.com/domains when you are ready to ship for real.
primer.key_insight: BARQUE treats email as a subprocess, not a protocol. The Pop CLI wraps Resend's HTTP API behind `--from/--to/--subject/--attach`, and the only secret that crosses the process boundary is `RESEND_API_KEY`.

visual.alt: A sequence diagram: Markdown file enters barque CLI, barque CLI invokes pandoc+WeasyPrint to emit a PDF, then constructs a pop subprocess command with --from/--to/--subject/--attach flags and RESEND_API_KEY in its environment, then pop posts to Resend's HTTP API, then Resend delivers to the recipient mailbox.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'none'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated `_get_env_vars` block and can explain where `RESEND_API_KEY` enters the subprocess environment and why it never touches the Pop command-line arguments.

questions.prompt: Per `barque/core/email.py`, where does `RESEND_API_KEY` live at the moment BARQUE invokes the `pop` subprocess?
questions.explanation: email.py lines 231-232: `env["RESEND_API_KEY"] = self.config.resend_api_key`. The key is injected into the subprocess environment via the `env=` argument of `subprocess.run(...)` — the call begins at line 115 and the `env=` kwarg is on line 121. It is never passed as a command-line flag, where it would appear in process listings.

questions.prompt: BARQUE's `README.md` Email Setup section warns that free Resend accounts have a specific delivery constraint. What is it?
questions.explanation: README.md line 48: "Free Resend accounts can only send to your verified email (the one you signed up with). To send to any recipient, verify a domain at resend.com/domains." This is why BARQUE's default recipient in many configs is the author's own address — it works out of the box on a free tier.

questions.prompt: If you set `--provider smtp` instead of the default, which of these environment variables does BARQUE set for the Pop subprocess?
questions.explanation: email.py lines 236-243: the SMTP branch sets `POP_SMTP_HOST`, `POP_SMTP_PORT`, `POP_SMTP_USERNAME`, `POP_SMTP_PASSWORD`. `RESEND_API_KEY` is only set when `provider == EmailProvider.RESEND`, so choosing SMTP swaps the entire secret surface.
